elements.sent-2411-022: The primordial Universe was extremely hot and dense.
elements.sent-2411-023: Even after recombination (at $z=1100$), the gas in the Universe remained pressure supported against collapse and did not form stars.
elements.sent-2411-024: As the Universe expanded and cooled, dark matter structures began to form and gravitationally cluster both baryons and non-baryons.
elements.sent-2411-025: These pristine conditions were devoid of substantial metals to cool the gas and the elevated temperature of the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) provided a temperature floor to the Universe.
elements.sent-2411-026: Eventually, gas in these halos cooled sufficiently to undergo gravitational collapse.
elements.sent-2411-027: The fusion of hydrogen into helium in these first halos provided the "First Light" in the Universe, illuminating the dark ages and beginning the era of stars and galaxies in the Universe that we live in today.
elements.sent-2411-028: Theory ranges widely about when First Light occurred, requiring a sensitive balance to not form too early to increase the CMB electron scattering optical depth and not form too late to be unable to drive the observed cosmic reionization between redshifts of $5-10$.
elements.sent-2411-029: As such, theory broadly suggests that these first galaxies likely formed between redshifts of $15-25$.
elements.sent-2411-030: This broad range requires observations to constrain when first light occurred in the Universe.
elements.sent-2411-031: Observations have guided this picture at the bookends.
elements.sent-2411-032: At young end, precision cosmology using the CMB crucially constrains the temperature of the Universe during recombination, the density inhomogeneities that eventually led to large scale structures, and the timing of recombination.
elements.sent-2411-033: Meanwhile, over the past two centuries astronomical observations have peered progressively further away at galaxies and large-scale structures in the Universe to infer the accelerated expansion, the presence of dark matter, and the overall fundamental nature of the cosmos.
elements.sent-2411-034: Amazingly, with every new technological advancement (larger telescopes, improved sensitivity of astronomical imaging, and the advent of space-based instrumentation) has continued galaxies at larger and larger redshift, corresponding to earlier and earlier in cosmic time.
elements.sent-2411-035: Observations have not yet discovered galaxies during the epoch of First Light in the Universe.
elements.sent-2411-036: The JWST was specifically built to find the galaxies emitting the First Light in the Universe.
elements.sent-2411-037: These are exactly the wavelengths required to discover faint galaxies within First Light as the Lyman Break falls at 2.1~$\mu$m and 5~$\mu$m at redshifts 16 and 20, receptively.
elements.sent-2411-038: This means that broad imaging bands, such as the F200W and F277W can be paired together to search for continuum "drop outs" when the stellar continua of faint galaxies within First Light is absorbed by foreground neutral gas through the Ly$\alpha$ transition \citep[see e.g.][]{steidel96 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: This technique has successfully delivered the discovery of dozens of galaxies at $10<z<15$, a feat only imaginable with the predecessor of JWST - the Hubble Space Telescope.
elements.sent-2411-040: The $z > 10$ universe appears to have an over-abundance of bright galaxies that defies pre-JWST predictions \citep[to name just a few,][]{adams23,austin23,atek23a,curtislake23,donnan23,finkelstein23, castellano24, carniani24, robertson24 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: This over-abundance of bright galaxies may imply extremely efficient star formation within the first 400~Myr of cosmic time, bursty star formation histories, a complete lack of dust in these early galaxies, a super top-heavy initial mass (IMF) function, or accretion onto primordial black holes \citep[e.g.,][]{harikane23_agn,finkelstein23,pacucci22,ferrara23,boylan-kolchin23,sun23 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: While many previous observations have sought galaxies at even higher redshift in the First Light, few candidates pass rigorous scrutiny as brown-dwarfs, extremely dusty star-forming galaxies \citep{naidu22,zavala23,arrabal_haro23 , and spurious detections can masquerade as faint F200W drop-out galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Theory has rushed to incorporate the new astrophysics revealed at cosmic dawn into novel predictions of the population of First Light galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-044: These galaxies emerged so soon after the Big Bang such that their properties, abundance, and presence alone depend more sensitively on the detailed conditions of the early universe than any subsequent generation of galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-045: As such, galaxies during First Light are extremely sensitive to the clustering of halos and the properties of dark matter, the astrophysics of metal-free stellar populations, and the star formation efficiencies of nearly pristine hydrogen and helium gas.
elements.sent-2411-046: Here we report observations from a recently observed JWST observational campaign, JWST'S GLIMPSE.
elements.sent-2411-047: GLIMPSE was specifically aimed to target galaxies in First Light by pairing ultra-deep NIRCam imaging across seven wide and two medium bands with the natural lensing of the foreground galaxy cluster Abell S1063.
elements.sent-2411-048: With even modest magnifications factors of 2, the 30.6~mag GLIMPSE observations can reach absolute magnitudes ($M_{\rm UV}$) of $-18$, probing the faint galaxies that likely existed during First Light.
elements.sent-2411-049: We use these observations to reveal eight $z > 15$ candidates, observed within a single NIRCam pointing and scrutinize the validity of these candidates.
elements.sent-2411-050: We report the discovery of five galaxy candidates at redshifts between $15.9<z<18.6$ in \jwst\ observations from the GLIMPSE survey.
elements.sent-2411-051: These robust sources were identified using a combination of Lyman-break selection and photometric redshift estimates.
elements.sent-2411-052: The ultra-deep NIRCam imaging from GLIMPSE, combined with the strong gravitational lensing of the Abell S1063 galaxy cluster, allows us to probe an intrinsically fainter population (down to \muv $=-17.5$ mag) than previously achievable.
elements.sent-2411-053: These galaxies have absolute magnitudes ranging from \muv $= -17.7$ to $-18.0$ mag, with UV continuum slopes between $\beta \simeq -2.3$ and $\beta \simeq -3.0$, consistent with young, dust-free stellar populations.
elements.sent-2411-054: The number density of these objects, log$_{\rm 10}$ ($\phi$/[Mpc$^{-3}$ mag$^{-1}$])=$-3.43^{+0.28 _{-0.64}$ at $M_{\rm UV =-18$ is in clear tension with pre-\jwst\ theoretical predictions, extending the over-abundance of galaxies from $z\sim10$ to $z\sim 18.6$.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: These results, together with the scarcity of brighter galaxies in other public surveys, suggest a steep decline in the bright-end of the UV luminosity function at $z \sim 17$, implying efficient star formation and possibly a close connection to the halo mass function at these redshifts.
elements.sent-2411-056: Testing a variety of star formation histories suggests that these sources are plausible progenitors of the unusually UV-bright galaxies that JWST now routinely uncovers at $z = 10-14$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Overall, our results indicate that the luminosity distribution of the earliest star-forming galaxies could be shifting towards fainter luminosities, implying that future surveys of cosmic dawn will need to explore this faint luminosity regime.

elements.sent-2411-086: Here we report on observations from JWST'S GLIMPSE survey (Atek et al., 2025, in prep.).
elements.sent-2411-087: GLIMPSE was specifically aimed to detect galaxies during the epoch of first light, by pairing ultra-deep NIRCam imaging across seven wide and two medium bands with the gravitational lensing of the foreground galaxy cluster Abell S1063.

elements.sent-2411-277: In this study, we leverage ultra-deep GLIMPSE NIRCam imaging covering the AS1063 lensing cluster to identify a unique sample of high-redshift ($z > 16$) galaxy candidates.
